---
title: "Linear algebra in NumPy"
module: "M2 — Math You Actually Need"
chapter: 15
difficulty: "intermediate"
prereqs:
  - "M2 Ch5 — Dot products & similarity"
  - "M1 Ch12 — A NumPy preview"
learning_objectives:
  - "Represent vectors and matrices as NumPy arrays and read their `.shape`"
  - "Compute dot products and matrix multiplication with `@`"
  - "Transpose with `.T` and apply a matrix to a vector"
compute: "browser"
status: "stable"
last_reviewed: "2026-06-29"
est_minutes: 16
resources:
  - ../../lib/grader.py
review_cards:
  - id: at-operator
    q: "What does the `@` operator do in NumPy?"
    a: "It computes the dot product of two vectors, or matrix multiplication for 2-D arrays — the sum-of-products operation, not element-by-element multiplication."
  - id: shape-attr
    q: "What does an array's `.shape` tell you?"
    a: "The size along each dimension as a tuple: a length-3 vector is `(3,)` and a 2-row, 3-column matrix is `(2, 3)`."
  - id: transpose-T
    q: "What does `.T` (transpose) do to a matrix?"
    a: "It flips rows and columns, so an element at row i, column j moves to row j, column i; a `(2, 3)` matrix becomes `(3, 2)`."
format:
  live-html:
    theme:
      light:
        - cosmo
        - ../../theme/larnix.scss
      dark:
        - darkly
        - ../../theme/larnix-dark.scss
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    code-copy: true
    code-overflow: wrap
    include-after-body: ../../theme/_after-body.html
execute:
  enabled: false
---

{{< badge difficulty=intermediate >}} {{< badge compute=browser >}} {{< badge status=stable >}}

## The hook

Chapters 2 through 5 were pictures and arrows: a vector as a point, a matrix as a
grid that moves points around, a dot product as a way to measure similarity. Those
pictures built the intuition. But a model never draws an arrow — when it needs a
dot product or a matrix multiply, it calls **NumPy**, millions of times a second.

This chapter does the same operations you already understand, but in real code, in
one tool. Nothing new conceptually — you are translating the pictures into the lines
a model actually runs.

> You'll need from before: dot products and similarity (M2 Ch5), and the NumPy
> array basics (M1 Ch12). That's the toolkit we build on here.

---

## Vectors and matrices are arrays

In NumPy, a **vector** is a 1-D array and a **matrix** is a 2-D array (a list of
rows). You build both with `np.array`:

```{pyodide}
import numpy as np

v = np.array([1, 2, 3])           # a vector: 3 numbers in a row
M = np.array([[1, 2, 3],          # a matrix: 2 rows, 3 columns
              [4, 5, 6]])

print("v =", v)
print("M =")
print(M)
print("v.shape =", v.shape)       # (3,)   -> a length-3 vector
print("M.shape =", M.shape)       # (2, 3) -> 2 rows, 3 columns
```

The `.shape` attribute is the single most useful thing to read off an array. It is
a tuple giving the size along each dimension:

- `v.shape` is `(3,)` — one dimension, length 3. (The trailing comma is Python's
  way of writing a one-element tuple; it is not a typo.)
- `M.shape` is `(2, 3)` — two dimensions: 2 rows then 3 columns. The convention is
  always `(rows, columns)`.

When code breaks in real machine learning, it is almost always a shape that doesn't
line up. Reading `.shape` is your first debugging move, every time.

## Element-wise is not the dot product

Multiply two arrays with `*` and NumPy multiplies them **position by position** —
this is *element-wise*, and it gives back an array the same shape as the inputs.
That is not the dot product. The dot product multiplies position by position *and
then adds the results into a single number*.

```{pyodide}
import numpy as np

a = np.array([1, 2, 3])
b = np.array([4, 5, 6])

print("a * b   =", a * b)        # [ 4 10 18 ]  -> element-wise, still a vector
print("sum     =", np.sum(a * b))# 32           -> add them up by hand
print("np.dot  =", np.dot(a, b)) # 32           -> the dot product directly
print("a @ b   =", a @ b)        # 32           -> the same, with the @ operator
```

All three middle lines agree: `1·4 + 2·5 + 3·6 = 4 + 10 + 18 = 32`. The
`@` operator is the one you'll see most in real code — it is Python's dedicated
"matrix multiply" operator, and for two vectors it computes exactly the dot product
from Ch5. `np.dot(a, b)` does the same job; `@` reads more cleanly when
expressions get longer.

## A matrix times a vector: apply a transformation

Back in Ch3, a matrix was a machine that takes a point and moves it. In NumPy that
machine is one `@`. Multiplying a matrix `M` by a vector `v` applies the
transformation `M` describes to the point `v`:

```{pyodide}
import numpy as np

# A matrix that scales x by 2 and y by 3.
M = np.array([[2, 0],
              [0, 3]])
v = np.array([4, 5])

result = M @ v
print("M @ v   =", result)       # [8 15] -> x doubled, y tripled
print("shape   =", result.shape) # (2,)   -> still a 2-D point
```

Read it row by row: the first output number is the first row of `M` dotted with
`v` (`2·4 + 0·5 = 8`), and the second is the second row dotted with `v`
(`0·4 + 3·5 = 15`). A matrix–vector product is **one dot product per row**.
That is the whole idea: a layer of a neural network takes its input vector and does
exactly this — a matrix times a vector — to produce the next vector.

The shape rule is worth saying out loud: a `(2, 2)` matrix times a `(2,)` vector
gives a `(2,)` vector. In general, multiplying a `(rows, k)` matrix by a length-`k`
vector gives a length-`rows` vector. The inner sizes must match.

## A matrix times a matrix

Stacking transformations means multiplying matrices together, again with `@`. Each
entry of the result is one row of the left matrix dotted with one column of the
right matrix:

```{pyodide}
import numpy as np

A = np.array([[1, 2],
              [3, 4]])
B = np.array([[5, 6],
              [7, 8]])

print("A @ B =")
print(A @ B)                     # [[19 22] [43 50]]
print("shape =", (A @ B).shape)  # (2, 2)
```

The top-left entry is row 0 of `A` dotted with column 0 of `B`:
`1·5 + 2·7 = 19`. Work the other three the same way and you get the full grid. For
matrix–matrix multiplication the rule is `(m, k) @ (k, n)` gives `(m, n)`: the two
inner numbers must match and they cancel, leaving the outer two as the result shape.

## Transpose: flipping rows and columns

The transpose, written `.T`, flips a matrix over its diagonal: rows become columns
and columns become rows. The element at row `i`, column `j` ends up at row `j`,
column `i`.

```{pyodide}
import numpy as np

M = np.array([[1, 2, 3],
              [4, 5, 6]])

print("M.shape   =", M.shape)    # (2, 3)
print("M.T =")
print(M.T)                       # [[1 4] [2 5] [3 6]]
print("M.T.shape =", M.T.shape)  # (3, 2)
```

Transpose shows up constantly: to make shapes line up for an `@`, or to read the
same data the other way around. One handy fact you'll meet again:
`(A @ B).T` equals `B.T @ A.T` — transposing a product reverses the order. You'll
verify that yourself in the stretch exercise.

## The identity matrix

One special matrix does nothing: the **identity**, ones on the diagonal and zeros
elsewhere. Build it with `np.eye(n)`. Multiplying any vector or matrix by the
identity returns it unchanged — it is the matrix equivalent of multiplying a number
by 1.

```{pyodide}
import numpy as np

I = np.eye(2)
v = np.array([5, 7])

print("I =")
print(I)                         # [[1. 0.] [0. 1.]]
print("I @ v =", I @ v)          # [5. 7.] -> v, unchanged
```

The identity is the reference point for talking about inverses and "undoing" a
transformation later. For now, it is a clean check that your `@` is doing what you
expect: an identity should always hand back exactly what it received.

---

## Key Takeaways

::: {.key-takeaways}
### Key Takeaways

1. A **vector** is a 1-D NumPy array and a **matrix** is a 2-D array; read
   `.shape` (as `(rows, columns)`) to know what you're holding.
2. `*` multiplies **element-wise**; `@` (and `np.dot`) computes the **dot product**
   for vectors and **matrix multiplication** for 2-D arrays.
3. A **matrix times a vector** applies a transformation — one dot product per row;
   the inner sizes must match.
4. `.T` **transposes** (flips rows and columns), and `np.eye(n)` is the
   **identity** matrix that leaves things unchanged.
:::

---

## Practice

Each exercise is auto-graded by `run_tests`, loaded for you from `lib/grader.py`.

### Exercise 1 — Guided (≈4 min) 🟢

Complete `dot(a, b)` so it returns the dot product of two vectors as a plain `int`.
Use `@` on NumPy arrays, then wrap the result in `int(...)`. Replace `____`, then
press **Run**.

```{pyodide}
#| setup: true
#| exercise: ex_dot
from lib.grader import run_tests
```

```{pyodide}
#| exercise: ex_dot
import numpy as np

def dot(a, b):
    """Return the dot product of vectors a and b as a plain int."""
    return ____  # TODO: int(np.array(a) @ np.array(b))

run_tests([
    ("3-vectors", dot([1, 2, 3], [4, 5, 6]), 32),
    ("orthogonal", dot([1, 0], [0, 1]), 0),
])
```

<details><summary>If you're stuck</summary>

Turn each list into an array with `np.array(...)`, combine them with `@` to get the
sum of products, then wrap the whole thing in `int(...)` so the result is a plain
Python integer rather than a NumPy number.
</details>

<details><summary>Show solution</summary>

```python
import numpy as np

def dot(a, b):
    return int(np.array(a) @ np.array(b))
```

`np.array(a) @ np.array(b)` multiplies the vectors position by position and adds
the results: `1·4 + 2·5 + 3·6 = 32`. The orthogonal pair `[1, 0]` and `[0, 1]`
share no direction, so their dot product is `0`. `int(...)` returns a plain
Python integer.
</details>

### Exercise 2 — Implement (≈8 min) 🟡

Write `matvec(M, v)` that applies matrix `M` to vector `v` and returns the result as
a plain Python **list**. Convert both inputs with `np.array`, multiply with `@`, and
turn the array back into a list with `.tolist()`. Complete the marked line.

```{pyodide}
#| setup: true
#| exercise: ex_matvec
from lib.grader import run_tests
```

```{pyodide}
#| exercise: ex_matvec
import numpy as np

def matvec(M, v):
    """Apply matrix M to vector v; return a plain Python list."""
    return ____  # TODO: (np.array(M) @ np.array(v)).tolist()

run_tests([
    ("identity keeps v", matvec([[1, 0], [0, 1]], [5, 7]), [5, 7]),
    ("scales axes", matvec([[2, 0], [0, 3]], [4, 5]), [8, 15]),
])
```

<details><summary>If you're stuck</summary>

The pattern mirrors Exercise 1: `np.array(M) @ np.array(v)` does the matrix–vector
multiply (one dot product per row of `M`). The result is a NumPy array, so call
`.tolist()` on it to hand back an ordinary Python list.
</details>

<details><summary>Show solution</summary>

```python
import numpy as np

def matvec(M, v):
    return (np.array(M) @ np.array(v)).tolist()
```

Each output entry is one row of `M` dotted with `v`. With the identity matrix every
row picks out one element of `v` unchanged, so `[5, 7]` comes straight back. The
scaling matrix `[[2, 0], [0, 3]]` doubles the first entry and triples the second:
`[8, 15]`. `.tolist()` converts the NumPy array to a plain list.
</details>

### Exercise 3 — Stretch (optional, ≈8 min) 🔴

Open-ended, **rubric-graded** (not auto-checked). Build two 2×2 matrices `A` and
`B` as NumPy arrays, compute `A @ B`, and verify the identity
`(A @ B).T == B.T @ A.T` — transposing a product reverses the order. Print both
sides and compare them.

**Rubric — a good answer:**

- Builds two 2×2 arrays with `np.array` and computes `A @ B` with `@`.
- Prints `(A @ B).T` and `B.T @ A.T` so the two sides can be read side by side.
- Confirms they are equal with `np.array_equal(...)` and prints the `True` result.

<details><summary>If you're stuck</summary>

Pick any numbers for `A` and `B`. Compute the left side as `(A @ B).T` and the
right side as `B.T @ A.T`. To compare two whole arrays for equality, use
`np.array_equal(left, right)` — comparing with plain `==` gives an array of
True/False rather than a single answer.
</details>

<details><summary>Show one possible answer</summary>

```python
import numpy as np

A = np.array([[1, 2],
              [3, 4]])
B = np.array([[5, 6],
              [7, 8]])

left = (A @ B).T
right = B.T @ A.T

print("(A @ B).T =")
print(left)
print("B.T @ A.T =")
print(right)
print("equal?", np.array_equal(left, right))
```

Both sides print the same grid, and `np.array_equal` returns `True`: transposing a
product flips the order of the factors. This identity is why you'll often see
`.T` rearranged across an `@` when lining up shapes in later chapters.
</details>

---

## Check yourself

{{< quiz quiz-ch15.yml >}}

---

## Where this fits / what's next

You've moved from pictures to running code: arrays for vectors and matrices,
`.shape` to read them, `@` for dot products and matrix multiplies, and `.T` to flip
rows and columns. These are the exact operations a model runs at every layer. Next
you'll assemble them into a single artificial **neuron** — its forward pass written
written in the NumPy you practiced here.
